# Method of approach

## Overview

This project implements a Coding Agent-centered mutation evaluation harness (the
"Coding Agent MRT") that measures how coding agents respond to graded source
code mutations. The core idea is simple: produce a clean baseline script,
create one or more mutated forks of that script, and run parallel agent
workflows to measure whether the agents detect, repair, or otherwise
compensate for mutations. The implementation draws on established
techniques from code representation [@alon2019code2vec], pre-trained
code models [@feng2020codebert], automated testing for ML systems
[@pei2017deepxplore], and mutation-testing analysis [@wong1998theoretical;
@debroy2010using].

Figure 1 shows the high-level mutation-fork pipeline used throughout the
experiments. The original `script.py` is forked into a mutated and an
original branch. Each branch is executed in an isolated agent context; the
agent may attempt repairs or adjustments before producing a final script
that is compared for functional equivalence and behavior consistency.

![](images/mutation_pipeline.svg){fig-alt="Mutation fork pipeline"}

Figure 1: LLM-Mutator mutation-fork evaluation pipeline. The pipeline
creates a mutated fork, runs two agent workflows in parallel, and
compares final outputs for behavioral and functional differences.

## Development environment and tools

- Implementation: Python 3.10+ (project includes a `pyproject.toml` and
	`requirements.txt` under the LLM-Mutator workspace). The reference
	implementation used a local virtual environment with packages listed in
	`requirements.txt` in the attached `Coding Agent MRT` folder.
- LLMs / Agents: experiments used off-the-shelf GPT-style models (via a
	local adapter or API) and smaller, reproducible code models for rapid
	iteration (CodeBERT-style encoders for representation experiments).
- Testing harness: pytest for unit checks and a custom runner to execute
	forks in isolated sandboxes.
- Versioning & reproducibility: each fork is saved with deterministic
	metadata (seed, mutation operator, timestamp) enabling reproducible
	experiments.

## OPENCODE subagent workflow (Coding Agent MRT)

In this repository OPENCODE is implemented as a local subagent living in
the `.opencode/` directory (see `opencode.json`, `package.json`, and the
`agents/` folder). The OPENCODE component in this project exposes a
file-driven / CLI-oriented interface rather than a remote RPC
service. Mutation requests are expressed as small JSON manifests or CLI
invocations; OPENCODE applies AST-aware edits and writes mutated
artifacts plus a signed manifest under `.opencode/agents/<run-id>/`.

Key implementation details that match the repo layout attached:

- OPENCODE artifacts: `.opencode/opencode.json`, `.opencode/package.json`,
  and `.opencode/agents/` contain the subagent configuration and produced
  mutation artifacts.
- Mutation testing and test evaluation use the Python toolchain in the
  repository: `pytest` drives the test-suite (`tests/test_numerical_integration.py`)
  and `mutmut` is available for additional mutation analysis (cache in
  `.mutmut-cache`).
- The driver harness runs OPENCODE via its CLI or by writing a request
  manifest that OPENCODE watches; mutated files are produced in-place in
  a quarantined copy of `src/` and the test-suite is executed with
  `pytest` to determine regressions.

The experimental flow is therefore:

1. The driver writes a mutation request (or invokes the OPENCODE CLI)
	containing the target path in `src/`, selected operator id, and a
	deterministic seed.
2. OPENCODE produces a quarantined mutated copy under
	`.opencode/agents/<run-id>/` and writes a manifest describing the
	operator, file location, and provenance (timestamp, seed, toolchain).
3. The harness runs `pytest` against the mutated copy to determine
	regressions. Optionally `mutmut` is used for fine-grained mutation
	coverage analysis and to cache mutation artifacts in `.mutmut-cache`.
4. Two agent contexts are created for evaluation: one for the clean script and one for the mutated script. Each agent runs in isolation, with the mutated agent unaware of the mutation.
5. The agents execute their respective scripts, and the outputs are collected.
6. The Comparator and Metrics Collector component evaluates the outputs for functional equivalence, using the defined metrics (detection rate, time-to-repair, etc.) to quantify robustness
	
This file-based, CLI-driven approach matches the repository structure in
the attached `Coding Agent MRT` folder and ensures reproducible runs by
preserving manifests and mutated artifacts under `.opencode/agents/`.

## Mutation operators and severity levels

Mutations are organized by semantic severity to exercise different
failure modes. Examples implemented in the Coding Agent MRT include:

- Structural removals: delete an entire function or method (major).
- Control-flow changes: flip conditional predicates (major).
- Type or API perturbations: replace an API call with a different but
	syntactically valid call (moderate).
- Cosmetic/no-op mutations: rename local variables, re-order imports,
	or insert dead code (minor) [@papadakis2019analysis].

Each mutation is applied deterministically using a scripted operator so
that experiments are repeatable. The severity levels are designed to create a gradient of difficulty for the agent, with the expectation that more severe mutations will be harder to detect and repair.

## System architecture and workflow

The system is logically divided into three components: (1) the
Mutation Engine, (2) the Agent Runner, and (3) the Comparator and
Metrics Collector.

- Mutation Engine: accepts a clean script and applies the chosen
	operator(s), producing a mutated script plus metadata (operator id,
	location, severity). The engine reuses AST-based transformations for
	language-aware edits so that mutated files remain syntactically valid
	where intended.
- Agent Runner: spawns an isolated execution context for an agent and
	provides the script plus any test harness. The agent is directed to
	run, detect issues, and optionally propose a patch; both the agent's
	intermediate logs and the final repaired script are captured.
- Comparator & Metrics Collector: runs the same test-suite or
	specification on agent outputs and computes functional equivalence.

### OPENCODE integration details (repo-specific)

Because OPENCODE in this project is implemented as a local subagent,
the driver interacts with it via CLI or filesystem manifests. Typical
driver actions are:

- `write_request(manifest) -> .opencode/requests/<id>.json` (file to be
	picked up by OPENCODE) or `npm run mutate -- <manifest.json>` when
	invoking the Node-based subagent directly.
- OPENCODE produces `.opencode/agents/<id>/mutated/` and
	`.opencode/agents/<id>/manifest.json` which the harness reads to
	continue evaluation.

Storing mutated artifacts under `.opencode/agents/` and using
`pytest`/`mutmut` for evaluation ensures exact provenance and enables
replayable experiments across different agent models defined in
`AGENTS.md`.

## Data, tests, and evaluation metrics

Test inputs are small, deterministic examples designed to exercise the
script's primary functionality. Evaluation metrics include:


- Detection rate: whether the agent explicitly identifies mutation and
	characterizes its effect.
- Time-to-repair (to measure work needed
	for recovery).

These metrics follow conventions from mutation-testing literature and
automated testing for ML systems [@wong1998theoretical; @debroy2010using;
@pei2017deepxplore].

## Reproducibility and implementation notes

- The `Coding Agent MRT` workspace included with the project contains a
	lightweight runner and unit tests (see `tests/test_numerical_integration.py`
	as an example of the test pattern used).

## Ethics and safety considerations

Applying mutations to code may surface vulnerabilities or generate
dangerous behavior in automated systems. To mitigate risk, the
experiments are constrained to small, deterministic toy programs or
isolated components (no production or safety-critical systems). The
project documents responsible use, and experimental artifacts are
released with disclaimers and guidance for ethical research.

## Summary

This Methods chapter described the LLM-Mutator pipeline, the
implemented mutation operators, the agent-runner architecture, and the
metrics used to quantify agent robustness. The following chapters report
results using this harness and analyze failure modes that emerge across
mutation severities.
